entry.headline: BofA Deploys AI Meeting Workflow Across Merrill and Private Bank, Targeting Up to 4 Hours Saved Per Client Meeting
entry.the_so_what: BofA's full-scale embedding of AI-Powered Meeting Journey across Merrill and the Private Bank — not as a pilot but as front-line advisory infrastructure claiming up to four hours recovered per client interaction — means the productivity baseline for wirehouse-scale wealth management has been permanently reset, and any competitor CXO still treating meeting workflow automation as a roadmap item rather than a deployed capability is now defending an operating model that is structurally more expensive per advisor than BofA's.
entry.summary: Bank of America's AI-Powered Meeting Journey tool automates three core advisor workflow steps — pre-meeting client preparation, real-time meeting summarization (with client consent), and post-meeting task organization — and is now in full-scale rollout across Merrill Wealth Management and the Private Bank. Early users report workflow improvements, indicating the system is live in active client settings rather than in testing; the tool originated with approximately 1,500 beta users before this broader deployment. The claimed productivity gain is up to four hours saved per meeting, which at scale could meaningfully increase advisor capacity for high-value planning conversations and fee-based mandate development. The rollout positions this AI layer as embedded infrastructure in front-line advisory processes — comparable to competing deployments at JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, and Goldman Sachs — rather than a peripheral chatbot application. Key execution risks include advisor adoption breadth, integration reliability with platforms such as Salesforce and Zoom, and regulatory scrutiny over AI-generated summaries in a supervised wealth segment.

key_stat.label: Hours potentially saved per client meeting at full deployment scale across Merrill and Private Bank

capability_evidence.evidence: Early users of the AI-Powered Meeting Journey tool across Merrill Wealth Management and the Private Bank are already reporting workflow improvements in live client settings, following a beta with approximately 1,500 advisors.
capability_evidence.metric: Up to 4 hours saved per meeting

landscape_context.current_maturity: Full-scale deployment across two major wealth channels (Merrill Wealth Management and Private Bank), post-beta with approximately 1,500 initial users — indicating production-grade maturity rather than experimentation
landscape_context.competitor_gap: No peer comparison data available for this capability in this segment

_enrichment.notes: No prior BofA coverage and no peer landscape data available for advisor productivity capability benchmarking; competitive framing (JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs) referenced in summary but not supported by structured landscape data, so competitor gap cannot be quantified.

verified_claims: Bank of America has begun a full-scale rollout of its AI-Powered Meeting Journey tool across Merrill Wealth Management and the Private Bank
The tool automates pre-meeting client preparation (meeting materials)
The tool provides real-time meeting summarization acting as a notetaker with client consent
The tool organizes post-meeting follow-up tasks for advisors
Early users are already reporting workflow improvements, suggesting the system is live in active client settings rather than in testing
The tool originated with approximately 1,500 beta users before broader deployment
The claimed productivity gain is up to four hours saved per meeting
The rollout is positioned as an AI layer embedded in core advisory processes rather than a peripheral chatbot application
Competing deployments exist at JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, and Goldman Sachs

Key execution risks include advisor adoption breadth, integration reliability with platforms such as Salesforce and Zoom, and regulatory scrutiny over AI-generated summaries in a supervised wealth segment
If time savings are achieved, advisors could handle more complex planning conversations supporting deeper relationships and more fee-based mandates
The tool auto-generates meeting prep, acts as a notetaker with client consent, and turns discussions into tasks
unverified_claims: The claim that the productivity baseline for wirehouse-scale wealth management has been 'permanently reset' — this is editorial inference not stated in the source

The claim that competitors treating meeting workflow automation as a 'roadmap item' are 'structurally more expensive per advisor' — this is analytical inference not supported or contradicted by the source
The specific framing that this is 'not a pilot' — the source says 'full-scale rollout' but does not explicitly use the word 'pilot' to contrast against
_governance.notes: All core factual claims in the entry are directly supported by the source article; the only unverified claims are editorial/analytical inferences in the 'so what' section that go beyond what the source states but are not contradicted by it, warranting a REVIEW rather than PASS.

entry.headline: Jump's AI Operating System Reaches 27,000 Advisors, Expanding From Meeting Notes to Full Client Lifecycle Automation
entry.the_so_what: Jump has arrived in our coverage already post-category-creation — at 27,000 advisors, $80M in fresh capital, and a three-product lifecycle architecture launched simultaneously, any firm still evaluating point solutions for meeting notes is not behind on a feature decision, it is behind on an operating model decision.
entry.summary: Jump has advanced from a single AI meeting assistant to a full AI Operating System for Advisors, now comprising three integrated products — Meet, Grow, and Operate — built on a unified data, integration, and agentic AI orchestration layer that spans the entire client lifecycle. The platform converts client conversations, documents, and emails into structured intelligence, compliance-ready documentation, and automated workflows, enabling advisory firms to scale operations without adding headcount. With over 27,000 advisors already deployed on the platform, Jump launched the expanded product suite in March 2026, three weeks after closing an $80M Series B to fund the expansion. The new modular structure allows firms to adopt capabilities incrementally while sharing a common operating foundation across all products.

key_stat.label: Financial advisors actively using Jump's AI platform at time of product launch

capability_evidence.evidence: More than 27,000 advisors have adopted Jump to eliminate hours of manual work daily, with the expanded platform now automating meeting workflows, back-office operations, and organic growth activities through a unified agentic AI orchestration layer.

landscape_context.current_maturity: Full AI operating system with agentic orchestration across client lifecycle (Meet, Grow, Operate); post-Series B with 27,000 advisor deployments
landscape_context.competitor_gap: No peers at this maturity level yet in our landscape data

_enrichment.notes: Company not previously in landscape; no peer comparison data available for advisor_productivity segment — competitor gap assessment based solely on available landscape data, not independent market research.

_format_errors: week "2026-03-23" is not the Monday of date "2026-03-09" (expected "2026-03-09")

verified_claims: Jump has advanced from a single AI meeting assistant to a full AI Operating System for Advisors
The platform comprises three integrated products — Meet, Grow, and Operate
The products are built on a unified data, integration, and agentic AI orchestration layer
The system spans the entire client lifecycle
The platform converts client conversations, documents, and emails into structured intelligence, compliance-ready documentation, and automated workflows
The platform enables advisory firms to scale operations without adding headcount
Over 27,000 advisors have adopted Jump's AI platform
The new modular structure allows firms to adopt capabilities incrementally while sharing a common operating foundation
Jump raised an $80M Series B
The product launch was announced on March 9, 2026
unverified_claims: The expanded product suite was launched 'three weeks after closing an $80M Series B' — the Series B article is dated February 19, 2026 and the product launch March 9, 2026, which is approximately three weeks, but the source does not explicitly state this causal/temporal relationship

The 27,000 advisors figure is described in the entry as advisors 'actively using' the platform; the source says 'adopted Jump' over the past two years, which may or may not be equivalent to 'actively using at time of launch'
The claim that the $80M Series B was specifically intended 'to fund the expansion' is not explicitly stated in the visible source text
_governance.notes: All major claims are well-supported by the source; a few temporal and causal linkages between the Series B and product launch are plausible but not explicitly confirmed in the visible source text, warranting a REVIEW rather than PASS.
